title: Making Changes
subtitle: The edit-add-commit cycle

sections.content: Real development is a cycle: you make changes to your files, stage them with `git add`, and save them with `git commit`. This **edit → add → commit** rhythm is the core of working with git every day.
sections.analogy: Think of it like a daily journal routine. You write your thoughts during the day (edit files), choose which pages to keep (stage with `git add`), then bind them into the journal (commit). Tomorrow you do it again, and your journal grows one entry at a time.
keyPoints: The core git workflow is: edit files → `git add` → `git commit`
You can make many edits before staging and committing
Each commit should represent one logical change or task

sections.instruction: Let's practice the full git workflow: initialize, commit, make changes, commit again.

onSuccess.message: Files staged. Now save this as your starting point.

sections.prompt: The three-step git workflow is: edit files, ___, then commit.

sections.instruction: Create your first commit — a snapshot of the project as it is right now.

onSuccess.message: First commit saved. Now let's make a change and commit it too.

sections.instruction: Now make a change to your file. Add a welcome paragraph to the HTML file.
sections.prompt:
Type `echo "

Welcome to my site

" >> index.html` to append content.
sections.hint:
Type: echo "

Welcome to my site

" >> index.html

onSuccess.message: You modified the file. The `>>` operator appends text to the end of a file. Let's save this change as a new commit.

sections.instruction: Now save your change. Stage the modified file and commit it with a descriptive message.
sections.prompt: Stage and commit: first `git add .`, then `git commit -m "Add welcome paragraph"`.

onSuccess.message: Two commits now! You've built a history. Each commit is a save point you can return to. This is the core cycle: edit, add, commit. Edit, add, commit.

completionMessage: You've mastered the edit-add-commit cycle — the daily rhythm of every developer. Make changes, stage them, commit them. Next: viewing your project's history.
